findings.title: enableEdgeToEdge() in CrashReportActivity does not disturb FLAG_SECURE — verify ordering
findings.description: CrashReportActivity.onCreate currently calls window.setFlags(FLAG_SECURE, FLAG_SECURE) at line 83, immediately after super.onCreate. Task 6 specifies enableEdgeToEdge() must be added 'after the FLAG_SECURE setFlags, before setContent'. enableEdgeToEdge() and FLAG_SECURE operate on independent dimensions — edge-to-edge controls layout/inset behaviour and SystemBarStyle (decor fits + bar appearance), whereas FLAG_SECURE is a separate window flag bit (LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE = 0x2000) governing screenshot/screen-recording suppression by SurfaceFlinger. Calling enableEdgeToEdge() does not call setFlags or clearFlags, so the secure bit is preserved. However, the tech-spec should make the ordering invariant explicit and the audit task should grep for any future code that calls window.setFlags or window.clearFlags after enableEdgeToEdge that could clobber FLAG_SECURE.
findings.impact: If a future change calls window.attributes = ... or any LayoutParams replacement after enableEdgeToEdge(), FLAG_SECURE could be silently cleared, exposing crash content (which may include user prompts, partial responses, file paths, exception messages with PII) to screenshots and screen-recording apps including OEM Recents thumbnails.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit acceptance criterion to Task 6: 'After change, verify FLAG_SECURE is still set on CrashReportActivity at runtime — e.g. instrumented test or manual screenshot attempt. No call to window.setFlags / window.clearFlags / window.attributes assignment exists between enableEdgeToEdge() and end of onCreate.' Also add a grep guard in Task 8 (Code Audit) for setFlags/clearFlags on window after enableEdgeToEdge.

findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: Log injection surface — verify reset log message construction uses only enum.name and bounded fields
findings.description: Tech-spec Decision 5 and the reset-flow pseudocode use messages like 'reason=LIGHT_OVERRIDE' and 'skipped reason=LIGHT_OVERRIDE status=...'. The 'reason' part is enum-derived (ResetReason.name) and is therefore safe — enum constant names are compile-time strings of [A-Z_]+. However, 'status=...' is the ModelInitStatus value which may include a Failed wrapper carrying a Throwable.message. If the status formatting interpolates Throwable.message directly into the log line without sanitization, control whitespace from a native exception message (newlines, tabs) could break the one-line-per-event invariant of ErrorLog (line 21: CONTROL_WS regex is applied only inside ErrorLog.e's sanitize()). The tech-spec must require that the new ErrorLog.i / ErrorLog.w wrappers apply the same sanitize() and length-bound (500/200) as ErrorLog.e — Decision 5 says 'with the same length-bounding behaviour' but only Task 2 enforces it; Task 1's reset log call sites are not explicitly required to pass through sanitize().
findings.location: Section: Decision 5; Tech-spec § How it works (line 69 'skipped reason=LIGHT_OVERRIDE status=...'); Task 2
findings.impact: Log forgery / log injection: a Failed status whose Throwable.message contains a newline followed by a forged 'ERROR [download] fake event' line could pollute the diagnostic export with attacker-controlled events. Severity is minor because (a) the source of the Throwable is the LiteRT-LM native engine, not direct user input, and (b) the diagnostic file is on-device and only exported via SAF user action — but the message ultimately derives from model/engine inputs which include user prompts.
findings.recommendation: In Task 2, make explicit that ErrorLog.i and ErrorLog.w MUST share the buildLine + sanitize pipeline with ErrorLog.e (refactor: extract write(level, component, description, cause) and call from e/i/w). Add a unit test in ErrorLogTest: 'i_strips_newlines_in_description', 'w_truncates_long_description_to_500'. In Task 1, when constructing the skip-path message, route any non-enum value (status, throwable) through .toString() and rely on the writer's sanitize() — never pre-format with String.format that bypasses bounds.

findings.title: ErrorLog length-bounding contract must be explicitly preserved across new levels (i/w)
findings.description: ErrorLog.kt currently bounds description to 500 chars and cause.message to 200 chars (lines 14-15) and applies CONTROL_WS sanitization inside sanitize() at line 109-110. The bounds are only applied via the e() method's buildLine -> sanitize call chain. Task 2 adds i() and w() methods with 'the same length-bounding behaviour'. The current code structure does not factor sanitize/buildLine such that new methods automatically inherit those bounds — a naive copy-paste of e() that calls sanitize() works, but a wrapper that just appendText(line) or builds a different prefix (e.g., 'INFO' / 'WARN' instead of 'ERROR') could accidentally skip sanitization. Tech-spec should require structural refactor (single private write() helper) rather than three near-duplicates.
findings.impact: A future addition of an i/w-level call site that bypasses sanitize() could write multi-line entries breaking log parsers, blow the 2 MB rotation budget faster than expected, or carry control characters into the diagnostic export. The 2 MB cap absorbs bulk volume but a single unbounded entry undermines the per-line invariant relied upon by SAF export consumers.
findings.recommendation: In Task 2, mandate the refactor: extract a private `suspend fun write(level: String, component: String, description: String, cause: Throwable?)` that does whitelist check, sanitize, length-bound, append, rotate. Public e(), i(), w() each call write(level=...). Add a test 'i_and_w_share_e_length_bounds' that constructs 1000-char descriptions and asserts the persisted line for each level is ≤ MAX_DESCRIPTION_LEN + prefix.

findings.title: Reset reason logging records chat-switch behavioural pattern — minor on-device privacy consideration
findings.description: Every chat switch, draft commit, light-override slider apply, system-prompt change, heavy reinit and user-tap reset now writes a line to errors.log with reason tag and timestamp (implicit via filesystem mtime / log file order). Aggregated, this is a behavioural fingerprint: 'user switches chats every 90s', 'user adjusts temperature 14 times per session', etc. No content is logged — only the categorical reason and engine status. The log file lives in app-internal storage (context.filesDir, line 84) which is sandboxed by Android and not world-readable on non-rooted devices. Export happens only via explicit user-initiated SAF flow (CrashReportActivity export). This is acceptable for on-device diagnostics, but the tech-spec does not state retention behaviour and there is no log redaction at export time. GDPR Art.5(1)(c) data-minimization argues for not collecting more than needed.
findings.impact: Low. On-device only, sandboxed storage. Risk surface: (a) user shares a diagnostic export to support and inadvertently reveals usage cadence; (b) device backup (auto-backup is enabled by default unless allowBackup=false) could replicate logs to Google Drive. The reset-reason category set is small (6 enum values) and intrinsically non-sensitive, but timing patterns can leak.
findings.recommendation: Add to tech-spec acceptance criteria a one-line confirmation that errors.log path is excluded from auto-backup (verify android:fullBackupContent / dataExtractionRules in AndroidManifest excludes filesDir/logs, or that allowBackup=false at app level). If not already excluded, add a backup_rules.xml exclusion for files-path 'logs/'. Optionally: document in patterns.md § ErrorLog that 'reset' lines exist and are user-visible via export — so the user understands what gets exported. No code change required if backup is already disabled or scoped.

findings.title: lifecycleMutex serialization invariant for resetConversation is asserted in tests but not in static analysis
findings.description: Decision: resetConversation is serialized by lifecycleMutex (Shared Resources table line 97). Tech-spec adds new call sites: applyLightOverrides, bootstrapChatModelId post-Ready callback, plus existing SYSTEM_PROMPT and HEAVY paths. The unit test 'resetConversation_isSerializedByLifecycleMutex' verifies serialization for two parallel calls, but does not cover the interaction between resetConversation and concurrent initialize/cleanup. If a chat-switch fires resetConversation while a Heavy-tier max_tokens change is mid-flight (cleanup running), the new caller would block on lifecycleMutex — correct behaviour — but the warning-log path triggered when status != Ready could fire mid-cleanup with status=Initializing and emit a misleading 'skipped reason=CHAT_SWITCH status=Initializing' line. This is operationally noisy, not a security vuln, but it is an integrity-of-diagnostics concern: a diagnostic line claims a reset 'was attempted and skipped' when the user's intent (CHAT_SWITCH) is actually queued behind an unrelated cleanup.
findings.impact: Diagnostic clarity / debuggability. Not a confidentiality or integrity issue against an attacker. A future debugger triaging 'why did chat switch leak KV?' might be misled by a 'skipped' line that was actually a race-with-Heavy artifact.
findings.recommendation: Add to Task 1 description: when resetConversation observes status != Ready, the warning message must include the in-flight operation if known (e.g., 'skipped reason=CHAT_SWITCH status=Initializing inflight=heavy-reinit'), or alternatively retry once after the mutex acquire completes. Simpler: document in patterns.md D15 that 'skip' lines next to a cleanup/initialize event indicate a race, not user-facing breakage. No security-critical fix required.
